OBL-001.description: Material cybersecurity incident disclosure on Form 8-K (Item 1.05)
criteria: The 4-day clock starts at the materiality determination, not at the date the incident was discovered or detected
Materiality is assessed using a reasonable person or reasonable investor standard, not a fixed numerical threshold
When uncertain whether an incident is material, companies should favor disclosure rather than withholding
The materiality determination process cannot be unreasonably delayed in order to avoid or postpone the filing deadline
A national security exception exists that allows the company to delay the Form 8-K filing
The disclosure covers basic identifying details and the material impact of the incident, not full remediation details
Cross-functional coordination is required involving cybersecurity, legal, finance, and disclosure teams
The scope of coverage includes incidents on third-party systems, not only systems owned by the registrant
Registrants are not required to disclose specific technical information that could reveal vulnerabilities or impair incident response or remediation
A series of related unauthorized occurrences can collectively meet the materiality threshold and trigger a single Form 8-K
The Form 8-K must address both the nature, scope, and timing of the incident AND its material impact on the company's financial condition and results of operations
Intentionally deferring committee meetings beyond typical timeframes or altering incident response policies to extend the determination timeline constitutes unreasonable delay
The national security delay can be extended in stages up to a total of 120 days in extraordinary circumstances
Failure to timely file the Form 8-K for a material cybersecurity incident does not affect the company's Form S-3 or Form SF-3 shelf registration eligibility

OBL-002.description: Describe material aspects of the incident in Form 8-K
criteria: The disclosure must cover the nature and scope of the incident
The rule does NOT require disclosure of whether the incident is ongoing, its remediation status, or whether data was compromised (these were proposed but dropped in the final rule)
The disclosure must describe the material impact or reasonably likely material impact on the company
Companies may file with incomplete information and amend the Form 8-K within four business days once that information becomes available
The company must seek missing information without unreasonable delay before filing the amendment

OBL-003.description: Form 8-K amendment when initially unavailable information becomes available
criteria: An amendment must be filed within four business days of the registrant determining or obtaining previously unavailable information
The amendment obligation is triggered when information was not known or available at the time of the initial Form 8-K filing
The company must actively seek missing information without unreasonable delay rather than waiting passively

OBL-006.description: Annual cybersecurity risk management, strategy, and governance disclosure (Item 1C / Item 106)
criteria: Companies must disclose their processes for assessing, identifying, and managing material risks from cybersecurity threats
Disclosure must state whether cybersecurity risk processes are integrated into the company's overall enterprise risk management system
Disclosure must state whether the company engages third-party assessors, consultants, auditors, or other parties in its cybersecurity risk processes
Disclosure must cover how the company identifies and manages material cybersecurity risks arising from its use of third-party service providers
Companies must explain whether cybersecurity risks have materially affected or are reasonably likely to materially affect business strategy, results of operations, or financial condition
Board oversight of cybersecurity risk must be disclosed, including which specific board committee or subcommittee is responsible
Disclosure must describe the processes by which the board or committee is informed about cybersecurity threats and incidents
Management's role and relevant expertise in assessing and managing cybersecurity risk must be disclosed
Relevant management expertise includes prior work experience in cybersecurity, degrees or certifications, and other cybersecurity knowledge or background
Disclosure must describe whether and how management reports cybersecurity information up to the board or board committee
The disclosure must cover how cybersecurity risks are integrated into the overall risk management strategy

OBL-007.description: Inline XBRL tagging of cybersecurity disclosures
criteria: XBRL tagging requirement begins one year after the initial compliance date for the respective disclosure
The tagging requirement applies to both the Form 8-K incident disclosures and the Form 10-K annual cybersecurity disclosures

OBL-008.description: Foreign private issuer cybersecurity disclosure obligations
criteria: Foreign private issuers must furnish material cybersecurity incidents on Form 6-K when those incidents are disclosed or publicized in a foreign jurisdiction or to any stock exchange
Foreign private issuers must disclose board oversight of cybersecurity risks and management's role in the Form 20-F annual report
FPI compliance dates align with domestic registrant effective dates
